title: Kids Lounge Bench
summary: A low toddler-scale lounge bench built entirely from 1x3 and 1x2 slats. Small enough for a toddler to climb, sturdy enough for school-age kids. All brad-nailed.
description:
This is a low, slatted lounge bench sized for children — the source builder's 18-month-old could climb up on it, but it's big and sturdy enough for school-age kids too. It's built entirely from 1x3 and 1x2 boards, slatted like a little settee, and it goes together with a brad nailer and glue with no complicated joinery.

The seat sits at 12", with the seat support placed 11-1/4" off the ground. A couple of refinements from the builder are worth adopting: add an extra 1x2 across the back of the middle support for the backrest to lean against (it makes assembly easier and the back more solid), and put the back-support 1x2 on the inside of the armrests rather than at the very back — it sits the seat back up a little straighter. The top support goes about 2" down from the top.

One assembly-order tip from experience: add the side 1x2s after you've put the back on and in place, because they tend to overlap and it's easier to trim them to fit last. Everything is brad-nailed and glued; sand well since little hands and legs will be all over it. It's an approachable build with a lot of repeated cuts, so a stop block on the miter saw pays off. Finish or paint in a child-safe coating.

tools.note: Lots of repeated short crosscuts — use a stop block.
Kids' skin against the slats — sand everything.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four 1x3 uprights at 18-1/4", eleven 1x3 back slats at 19", two 1x3 arm parts at 15-3/4", two 1x3 long rails at 31-1/2", three 1x3 seat front/back at 33", eleven 1x3 seat slats at 12-1/4" and two 1x3 side pieces at 17-1/4" from the 1x3. Cut two 1x2 top rails at 33", one 1x2 back support at 36-1/2", two 1x2 seat supports at 31-1/2" and two 1x2 side supports at 15-3/4" from the 1x2. That is 35 pieces of 1x3 totalling 644-3/4" and seven pieces of 1x2 totalling 197", which fit the seven 8 ft 1x3 and three 8 ft 1x2 this plan lists — but only if you mix different lengths on the same board instead of cutting one length at a time. A layout that closes with a 1/8" kerf is: two boards of 33 + 31-1/2 + 18-1/4 + 12-1/4, two boards of five 19s each, one board of 19 + 33 + 18-1/4 + 18-1/4, one board of 17-1/4 + 17-1/4 + 15-3/4 + 15-3/4 + 12-1/4 + 12-1/4, and a last board of seven 12-1/4s. Set a stop block on the miter saw for each repeated length, square every cut, and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Set the seat height and supports
steps.body: The bench is 33" wide overall: the 33" pieces run the full width and the 31-1/2" pieces fit between the 3/4" side assemblies, since 31-1/2" plus 3/4" at each end comes to 33". Fix the two 31-1/2" 1x2 seat supports between the sides with their top edges 11-1/4" off the ground — the 3/4" seat slats that land on them bring the finished seat to the intended 12" height. Add the extra 1x2 across the back of the middle support for the backrest to lean against; it makes assembly much easier and gives the back something solid to bear on instead of relying on the slat nails alone. Glue every joint and drive two 1-1/4" brads at each end, checking the two supports are level with each other before you commit — a seat support that is out of level shows up in every slat you lay on it.

steps.title: Position the back support
steps.body: Put the back-support 1x2 on the inside face of the armrests rather than at the very back — it sits the seat back up a little straighter and looks better than mounting it flush to the rear. Note before you fit it that this piece is cut at 36-1/2", which is 3-1/2" longer than the bench's 33" outside width, so it cannot sit between the sides the way the other 1x2 supports do; it has to run across and past them, or be trimmed to suit. The plan does not settle which, so hold it in place against your assembled sides and decide before nailing. Glue and 1-1/4" brads once you have it where you want it.

steps.title: Add the top support
steps.body: The top support is one of the two 33" 1x2 top rails, so like the 33" seat front/back it runs the full outside width and its ends land on the outer faces of the uprights. Set it about 2" down from the top of the 18-1/4" uprights and mark that 2" on both uprights before you offer the rail up, so it goes on level rather than by eye. Glue and two 1-1/4" brads into each upright. On a bench this size you can skip a separate middle support — it holds up fine without one.

steps.title: Slat the seat and back
steps.body: Lay the eleven 12-1/4" seat slats across the seat supports front to back, and stand the eleven 19" back slats up against the back supports. Eleven 2-1/2" slats come to 27-1/2" across the 31-1/2" clear span, which leaves 4" of gap spread over ten gaps — about 3/8" each — so cut a scrap spacer to that width and use it between every slat rather than eyeballing them; the eye catches an uneven gap instantly on a slatted piece. Glue each slat and drive two 1-1/4" brads into every support it crosses, keeping the outer slats flush with the frame at each end so the gaps stay symmetrical. One thing the source leaves open: the two 31-1/2" 1x3 long rails are on the cut list but no step installs them, so set them aside for now rather than guessing at a place for them.

steps.title: Add the side pieces last and finish
steps.body: Add the 17-1/4" 1x3 side pieces and the 15-3/4" 1x2 side supports last, after the back is on and in place — they tend to overlap the parts already fitted, so leaving them until the end lets you scribe and trim them to the real opening instead of a drawing. Fit the two 15-3/4" 1x3 arm parts the same way, holding each one in place and marking it rather than measuring twice. Glue and 1-1/4" brads throughout. Then fill every nail hole and sand the whole bench — edges, corners, slat ends and the undersides of the arms, because small hands find every splinter — and finish or paint in a child-safe coating, letting it cure fully before the bench goes into use.

images.alt: Low slatted lounge bench sized for young children
